pattern: Track only who currently holds each node's lock; ancestor and descendant checks are answered on demand by walking parent pointers upward or the children lists downward from the node in question, so no per-operation bookkeeping is needed beyond a single owner array.
hints: Precompute each node's children list from the parent array once, in the constructor; you'll need to walk both up (to the root) and down (to the leaves) from arbitrary nodes.

lock and unlock only ever look at the single node's own owner; the interesting work is upgrade, which needs two independent checks: does any strict ancestor currently hold a lock, and does any strict descendant currently hold a lock.

For upgrade: first confirm the node itself is unlocked, then walk up via parent pointers checking for any locked ancestor (fail fast if found), then walk down the children tree (DFS/BFS) collecting every currently-locked descendant. If that collection is non-empty, unlock all of them, lock the node for the new user, and succeed; otherwise fail without changing anything.

edge_inputs.description: statement example: lock, a failed unlock by the wrong user, a successful unlock, then an upgrade that unlocks a descendant
upgrade must fail when the node itself is already locked, even if it has locked descendants and no locked ancestors
upgrade must fail when a strict ancestor is locked, even though a descendant is also locked
upgrade must fail when the node has no locked descendants at all, even though it is unlocked with no locked ancestor
unlock fails for a user who never held the lock, and for a node that is already unlocked
two-node tree, the minimum size allowed by the constraints
a successful upgrade with multiple locked descendants at different depths unlocks all of them at once

stress.description: random tree of ~150 nodes with a long randomized mix of lock/unlock/upgrade calls among a small pool of users to force contention
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = 150
    parent = [-1] + [rng.randint(0, i - 1) for i in range(1, n)]
    ops = ['LockingTree']
    args = [[parent]]
    methods = ['lock', 'unlock', 'upgrade']
    for _ in range(max(1, size)):
        m = rng.choice(methods)
        num = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        user = rng.randint(1, 40)
        ops.append(m); args.append([num, user])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: a small tree with few users maximizes lock contention and exercises the ancestor/descendant checks in upgrade heavily
